content.text: What Is a Vision Encoder?

A vision encoder is the visual backbone of a multimodal large language model — the component that converts a raw image into a sequence of dense vector representations (visual tokens) that a language model can process alongside text tokens. Without it, the LLM has no way to perceive images; with it, the same transformer machinery that reads words can "read" visual content.

The design of the vision encoder shapes every downstream capability: its resolution determines fine-grained detail, its pre-training objective determines whether visual features align well with language, and its output dimensionality determines how many tokens the LLM must process per image. Choosing and configuring the right encoder is often the most impactful architectural decision in building a multimodal LLM.

attrs.alt: Vision encoder pipeline: image → patch split → linear projection → ViT transformer layers → visual tokens → connector/projector (linear, MLP, or Q-Former) → LLM → generated text, with table of common pre-trained encoders

content.text: From CNN to Vision Transformer

Early multimodal models used CNN feature extractors (ResNet, InceptionV3) that produced either global pooled vectors or spatial feature maps. These worked but had two structural problems: the feature map size was tied to the input resolution through a fixed stride pattern, and CNN features were not aligned with language — they were trained on ImageNet classification labels, not on paired image-text data.

The Vision Transformer (ViT, Dosovitskiy et al. 2020) resolved both problems. By splitting the image into a regular grid of fixed-size patches and treating each patch as a token, ViT produces a sequence of feature vectors that has the same structural form as a language token sequence — making it a natural fit for concatenation with text tokens in a unified transformer.
ViT Architecture: Patch Tokenisation
Given an image of height H and width W, ViT divides it into non-overlapping patches of size p × p pixels. Each patch is flattened into a vector of dimension

(C = 3 for RGB) and projected to the model's hidden dimension d via a learned linear layer. A learnable [CLS] token is prepended, and learnable 1D position embeddings are added to all tokens before the first transformer layer.

For a 224×224 image with patch size 16, N = 196. For 336×336 with patch size 14 (used by CLIP ViT-L/14@336), N = 576. The LLM must process all N visual tokens per image, so resolution and patch size directly control the computational cost.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PatchEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """Converts image to sequence of patch token embeddings."""
    def __init__(self, img_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, embed_dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_patches = (img_size // patch_size) ** 2
        self.patch_size = patch_size
        # Single Conv2d with kernel=stride=patch_size implements the split+project
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(
            in_channels, embed_dim,
            kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B, 3, H, W)
        x = self.proj(x)           # (B, embed_dim, H/p, W/p)
        x = x.flatten(2)           # (B, embed_dim, N)
        x = x.transpose(1, 2)      # (B, N, embed_dim)  ← token sequence
        return x

class ViTEncoder(nn.Module):
    """Minimal ViT encoder (for illustration; use pre-trained in practice)."""
    def __init__(self, img_size=224, patch_size=16, embed_dim=768, depth=12, heads=12):
        super().__init__()
        self.patch_embed = PatchEmbedding(img_size, patch_size, embed_dim=embed_dim)
        n = self.patch_embed.num_patches
        self.cls_token = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim))
        self.pos_embed = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, n + 1, embed_dim))
        self.blocks = nn.TransformerEncoder(
            nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(embed_dim, heads, dim_feedforward=embed_dim*4,
                                       batch_first=True, norm_first=True),
            num_layers=depth
        )
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        patches = self.patch_embed(x)                       # (B, N, d)
        cls = self.cls_token.expand(x.size(0), -1, -1)     # (B, 1, d)
        x = torch.cat([cls, patches], dim=1)               # (B, N+1, d)
        x = x + self.pos_embed
        x = self.blocks(x)
        return self.norm(x)  # (B, N+1, d) — all patch tokens + CLS
Pre-Training Objectives
The pre-training objective of the vision encoder critically determines whether its features are useful for language-driven tasks. Three objectives have dominated:
Contrastive (CLIP / SigLIP)

CLIP trains image and text encoders together by maximising the cosine similarity of matching pairs and minimising it for mismatched pairs within a batch. This directly aligns image features with language space — the single most important property for use in a multimodal LLM, since the visual tokens must be interpretable by a text-trained language model.
SigLIP

(Sigmoid Loss for Language-Image Pre-training, Zhai et al. 2023) replaces InfoNCE's softmax over the full batch with a per-pair sigmoid binary cross-entropy. This eliminates the global normalisation that makes InfoNCE hard to scale across very large batch sizes (which are critical for CLIP quality), and consistently outperforms CLIP at the same model size.
where
is the scaled image-text dot product and
is the label. Every pair is scored independently — no competition between pairs within the batch.
Reconstructive (EVA-CLIP)

EVA-CLIP (Sun et al. 2023) adds a reconstruction objective to contrastive training: the ViT must reconstruct the CLIP image embedding of masked patches, in addition to the standard contrastive loss. This forces the encoder to build dense, spatially-aware features rather than relying only on the [CLS] token, improving performance on tasks that require local visual understanding (grounding, dense captioning, segmentation).
Self-Supervised (DINOv2)

DINOv2 (Oquab et al. 2023) uses no language supervision at all. It applies self-distillation with masked patch reconstruction over large curated image datasets, producing features with exceptionally strong spatial properties (dense correspondence, segmentation quality). Its weakness for MLLM use is the lack of text alignment — DINOv2 features do not natively speak the same language as a text-trained LLM, requiring more adaptation.

# Loading pre-trained vision encoders with Hugging Face transformers
import torch
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel, CLIPImageProcessor
from PIL import Image

# CLIP ViT-L/14@336 — most common in LLaVA-1.5 style models
image_processor = CLIPImageProcessor.from_pretrained(
    "openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336"
)
vision_encoder = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained(
    "openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336"
).eval()

image = Image.open("photo.jpg")
pixel_values = image_processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = vision_encoder(pixel_values=pixel_values, output_hidden_states=True)

# All patch tokens (excluding CLS) from last hidden state
patch_tokens = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 1:, :]   # (1, 576, 1024)
cls_token    = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]    # (1, 1024)
print(f"Patch tokens: {patch_tokens.shape}")
print(f"CLS token:    {cls_token.shape}")

# Some models (e.g. BLIP-2) use the second-to-last hidden state
# because the last layer is fine-tuned for contrastive tasks
second_last = outputs.hidden_states[-2][:, 1:]        # (1, 576, 1024)
Connector Modules

Visual tokens from the encoder live in a different embedding space than text tokens. A connector (also called a projector or adapter) bridges the two, mapping encoder output into the LLM's input space. Three designs are in widespread use:
Connector
Architecture
Token count
Used in
Trade-off
Linear projection
Single nn.Linear: d_enc → d_llm
N (same as encoder)
LLaVA v1, MiniGPT-4
Cheapest; preserves all spatial info but passes many tokens to LLM
2-layer MLP
Linear → GELU → Linear
N (same as encoder)
LLaVA-1.5
Simple upgrade over linear; best accuracy-per-token ratio in practice
Q-Former
32 learnable queries + cross-attention over patch tokens
32 (fixed)
BLIP-2, InstructBLIP
Minimal LLM context usage; some spatial detail lost in compression
Resampler
Perceiver-style cross-attention to fixed number of latents
Flamingo, Idefics
Handles variable-length input elegantly; good for video
High-Resolution Strategies

Standard ViT encoders process images at 224–336px, which is insufficient for tasks requiring fine-grained visual detail: reading small text, counting objects, recognising handwriting, or examining medical scans. Two strategies extend resolution without retraining the encoder at higher resolution:
Image Tiling (LLaVA-NeXT / LLaVA-HD)

The high-resolution image is split into a grid of tiles (e.g. 2×2, 3×3), each resized to the encoder's native resolution, and encoded independently. The resulting patch-token grids are concatenated (with optional separator tokens) and fed to the LLM alongside a thumbnail encoding of the full image. This multiplies the token count by the number of tiles but preserves fine detail.

from PIL import Image
import torch

def tile_encode(image, encoder, processor, grid=(2, 2), tile_size=336):
    """
    Encode a high-res image as a grid of tiles + a full thumbnail.
    Returns: (B, (grid_h * grid_w + 1) * N_per_tile, d)
    """
    W, H = image.size
    gw, gh = grid
    tile_w, tile_h = W // gw, H // gh

    tile_tokens = []

    # Tile encoding
    for row in range(gh):
        for col in range(gw):
            tile = image.crop((
                col * tile_w, row * tile_h,
                (col + 1) * tile_w, (row + 1) * tile_h
            )).resize((tile_size, tile_size))
            pv = processor(images=tile, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values
            with torch.no_grad():
                feats = encoder(pixel_values=pv).last_hidden_state[:, 1:]
            tile_tokens.append(feats)  # (1, N, d)

    # Thumbnail encoding of full image
    thumb = image.resize((tile_size, tile_size))
    pv_thumb = processor(images=thumb, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values
    with torch.no_grad():
        thumb_feats = encoder(pixel_values=pv_thumb).last_hidden_state[:, 1:]
    tile_tokens.append(thumb_feats)

    return torch.cat(tile_tokens, dim=1)  # (1, (gw*gh+1)*N, d)
Native Resolution (NaViT / InternViT)

NaViT (Dehghani et al. 2023) modifies ViT to accept variable-length token sequences from different-sized images within the same batch, using 2D positional embeddings and masked attention. InternViT-6B trains a 6B-parameter ViT at 448×448 resolution directly. Both approaches are more elegant than tiling but require custom training rather than using existing pre-trained encoders.
Frozen vs. Fine-Tuned Encoders
Whether to freeze the vision encoder during MLLM training is a significant architectural choice:
Frozen encoder

(BLIP-2, LLaVA v1): only the connector and LLM are trained. Training is fast and cheap; the encoder's pre-trained features are preserved. The risk is that the encoder's features may not be optimally suited to the downstream task — it was trained on image-text pairs, not instruction-following.
Full fine-tuning

(LLaVA-1.5, InternVL-2): the encoder, connector, and LLM are all trained end-to-end (or with LoRA). Achieves the highest accuracy on dense prediction tasks but requires substantially more compute and risks catastrophic forgetting of the encoder's general visual knowledge.
LoRA on the encoder

: low-rank adapters (rank 4–16) are added to the encoder's attention layers. Only ~0.5% of the encoder's parameters are updated. This is the most common practical compromise: near-full fine-tuning quality with ~10× lower memory and gradient computation.

from peft import get_peft_model, LoraConfig, TaskType
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel

vision_encoder = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained(
    "openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336"
)

# Apply LoRA only to attention projection layers
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.FEATURE_EXTRACTION,
    r=16,                          # rank
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "out_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
)

vision_encoder_lora = get_peft_model(vision_encoder, lora_config)
vision_encoder_lora.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 4,325,376 || all params: 307,811,328 (1.4%)
Using a Pre-trained Encoder End to End
A minimal but complete example of extracting visual tokens and passing them to an LLM illustrates how all the components connect:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel, CLIPImageProcessor
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from PIL import Image

# --- Components ---
DEVICE = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

# 1. Vision encoder (frozen)
image_processor = CLIPImageProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336")
vision_model = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained(
    "openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336", torch_dtype=torch.float16
).to(DEVICE).eval()
for p in vision_model.parameters():
    p.requires_grad = False

# 2. LLM (e.g. TinyLlama for demonstration; use 7B+ in practice)
lm_tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0")
lm = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0", torch_dtype=torch.float16
).to(DEVICE)

# 3. 2-layer MLP connector (only trainable module)
vision_dim = vision_model.config.hidden_size          # 1024 for ViT-L/14
llm_dim    = lm.config.hidden_size                    # 2048 for TinyLlama
connector  = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(vision_dim, llm_dim),
    nn.GELU(),
    nn.Linear(llm_dim, llm_dim)
).to(DEVICE).to(torch.float16)

# --- Forward pass ---
image = Image.open("photo.jpg")
pixel_values = image_processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values.to(
    DEVICE, torch.float16
)

# Encode image → visual tokens
with torch.no_grad():
    visual_out = vision_model(pixel_values=pixel_values)
patch_tokens = visual_out.last_hidden_state[:, 1:]     # drop CLS: (1, 576, 1024)
visual_embeds = connector(patch_tokens)                 # (1, 576, 2048)

# Encode text prompt
prompt = "Describe what you see in the image."
text_input = lm_tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(DEVICE)
text_embeds = lm.get_input_embeddings()(text_input.input_ids)  # (1, T, 2048)

# Concatenate visual + text embeddings
combined = torch.cat([visual_embeds, text_embeds], dim=1)       # (1, 576+T, 2048)

# Generate
with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = lm.generate(
        inputs_embeds=combined,
        max_new_tokens=100,
        do_sample=False
    )
print(lm_tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Key Takeaways
ViT patch tokenisation is structurally compatible with LLMs
: image patches become token vectors of the same shape as word embeddings, enabling direct concatenation into the LLM's input sequence — the key insight that made modern MLLMs tractable.
Contrastive pre-training (CLIP/SigLIP) is preferred

: language-image alignment in the encoder's feature space makes visual tokens more interpretable by the LLM. Self-supervised encoders (DINOv2) have stronger spatial features but require more adaptation work.
Connector choice trades token count for expressiveness

: a linear or 2-layer MLP passes all N patch tokens (576 for 336px/14px) to the LLM — rich but expensive. Q-Former compresses to 32 fixed tokens — cheap but lossy. The 2-layer MLP (LLaVA-1.5 style) dominates current practice due to its accuracy-efficiency balance.
Resolution governs fine-grained detail

: standard 224px is inadequate for OCR, dense counting, and medical imaging. Tiling (processing sub-images at native resolution and concatenating their tokens) is the practical solution without retraining the encoder.
LoRA on the encoder is the best practical compromise

: full encoder fine-tuning improves dense-task accuracy but risks forgetting general visual knowledge. Freezing is cheapest but leaves performance on the table. LoRA updates ~1% of encoder parameters for near-full fine-tuning quality.
